title: Roman Statues WerenŌĆÖt White; They Were Once Painted in Vivid, Bright Colors
description: The idea of the clas┬Łsi┬Łcal periodŌĆöthe time of ancient Greece and RomeŌĆöas an ele┬Łgant┬Łly uni┬Łfied col┬Łlec┬Łtion of supe┬Łri┬Łor aes┬Łthet┬Łic and
body:
The idea of the clas┬Łsi┬Łcal periodŌĆöthe time of ancient Greece and RomeŌĆöas an ele┬Łgant┬Łly uni┬Łfied col┬Łlec┬Łtion of supe┬Łri┬Łor aes┬Łthet┬Łic and philo┬Łsoph┬Łi┬Łcal cul┬Łtur┬Łal traits has its own his┬Łto┬Łry, one that comes in large part from the era of the Neo┬Łclas┬Łsi┬Łcal. The redis┬Łcov┬Łery of antiq┬Łui┬Łty took some time to reach the pitch it would dur┬Łing the 18th cen┬Łtu┬Łry, when ref┬Łer┬Łences to Greek and Latin rhetoric, archi┬Łtec┬Łture, and sculp┬Łture were inescapable. But from the Renais┬Łsance onward, the clas┬Łsi┬Łcal achieved the sta┬Łtus of cul┬Łtur┬Łal dog┬Łma.
One tenet of clas┬Łsi┬Łcal ide┬Łal┬Łism is the idea that Roman and Greek stat┬Łu┬Łary embod┬Łied an ide┬Łal of pure whitenessŌĆöa mis┬Łcon┬Łcep┬Łtion mod┬Łern sculp┬Łtors per┬Łpet┬Łu┬Łat┬Łed for hun┬Łdreds of years by mak┬Łing busts and stat┬Łues in pol┬Łished white mar┬Łble. But the truth is that both Greek stat┬Łues and their Roman counterpartsŌĆöas youŌĆÖll learn in the Vox video aboveŌĆöwere orig┬Łi┬Łnal┬Łly bright┬Łly paint┬Łed in riotous col┬Łor.
This includes the 1st cen┬Łtu┬Łry A.D. Augus┬Łtus of Pri┬Łma Por┬Łta, the famous fig┬Łure of the Emper┬Łor stand┬Łing tri┬Łumphant┬Łly with one hand raised. Rather than left as blank white mar┬Łble, the stat┬Łue would have had bronzed skin, brown hair, and a fire-engine red toga. ŌĆ£Ancient Greece and Rome were real┬Łly col┬Łor┬Łful,ŌĆØ we learn. So how did every┬Łone come to believe oth┬Łer┬Łwise?
ItŌĆÖs part┬Łly an hon┬Łest mis┬Łtake. After the fall of Rome, ancient sculp┬Łtures were buried or left out in the open air for hun┬Łdreds of years. By the time the Renais┬Łsance began in the 1300s, their paint had fad┬Łed away. As a result, the artists unearthing, and copy┬Łing ancient art didnŌĆÖt real┬Łize how col┬Łor┬Łful it was sup┬Łposed to be. 
But white mar┬Łble couldnŌĆÖt have become the norm with┬Łout some will┬Łful igno┬Łrance. Even though there was a bunch of evi┬Łdence that ancient sculp┬Łture was paint┬Łed, artists, art his┬Łto┬Łri┬Łans and the gen┬Łer┬Łal pub┬Łlic chose to dis┬Łre┬Łgard it. West┬Łern cul┬Łture seemed to col┬Łlec┬Łtive┬Łly accept that white mar┬Łble was sim┬Łply pret┬Łti┬Łer. 
White stat┬Łu┬Łary sym┬Łbol┬Łized a clas┬Łsi┬Łcal ide┬Łal that ŌĆ£depends high┬Łly on the great┬Łest pos┬Łsi┬Łble decon┬Łtex┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łiza┬Łtion,ŌĆØ writes James I. Porter, pro┬Łfes┬Łsor of Rhetoric and Clas┬Łsics at the Uni┬Łver┬Łsi┬Łty of Cal┬Łi┬Łfor┬Łnia, Berke┬Łley. ŌĆ£Only so can the val┬Łues it cher┬Łish┬Łes be iso┬Łlat┬Łed: sim┬Łplic┬Łi┬Łty, tran┬Łquil┬Łi┬Łty, bal┬Łanced pro┬Łpor┬Łtions, restraint, puri┬Łty of formŌĆ” all of these are fea┬Łtures that under┬Łscore the time┬Łless qual┬Łi┬Łty of the high┬Łest pos┬Łsi┬Łble expres┬Łsion of art, like a breath held indef┬Łi┬Łnite┬Łly.ŌĆØ These ideals became insep┬Ła┬Łra┬Łble from the devel┬Łop┬Łment of racial the┬Ło┬Łry.
Learn┬Łing to see the past as it was requires us to put aside his┬Łtor┬Łi┬Łcal┬Łly acquired blind┬Łers. This can be exceed┬Łing┬Łly dif┬Łfi┬Łcult when our ideas about the past come from hun┬Łdreds of years of inher┬Łit┬Łed tra┬Łdi┬Łtion, from every peri┬Łod of art his┬Łto┬Łry since the time of Michelan┬Łge┬Łlo. But we must acknowl┬Łedge this tra┬Łdi┬Łtion as fab┬Łri┬Łcat┬Łed. Influ┬Łen┬Łtial art his┬Łto┬Łri┬Łan Johann Joachim Winck┬Łel┬Łmann, for exam┬Łple, extolled the val┬Łue of clas┬Łsi┬Łcal sculp┬Łture because, in his opin┬Łion, ŌĆ£the whiter the body is, the more beau┬Łti┬Łful it is.ŌĆØ
Winck┬Łel┬Łmann also, Vox notes, ŌĆ£went out of his way to ignore obvi┬Łous evi┬Łdence of col┬Łored mar┬Łble, and there was a lot of it.ŌĆØ He dis┬Łmissed fres┬Łcoes of col┬Łored stat┬Łu┬Łary found in Pom┬Łpeii and judged one paint┬Łed sculp┬Łture dis┬Łcov┬Łered there as ŌĆ£too prim┬Łi┬ŁtiveŌĆØ to have been made by ancient Romans. ŌĆ£Evi┬Łdence wasnŌĆÖt just ignored, some of it may have been destroyedŌĆØ to enforce an ide┬Łal of white┬Łness. While many stat┬Łues were denud┬Łed by the ele┬Łments over hun┬Łdreds of years, the first archae┬Łol┬Ło┬Łgists to dis┬Łcov┬Łer the Augus┬Łtus of Pri┬Łma Por┬Łta in the 1860s described its col┬Łor scheme in detail.
Cri┬Łtiques of clas┬Łsi┬Łcal ide┬Łal┬Łism donŌĆÖt orig┬Łi┬Łnate in a polit┬Łi┬Łcal┬Łly cor┬Łrect present. As Porter shows at length in his arti┬Łcle ŌĆ£What Is ŌĆśClas┬Łsi┬ŁcalŌĆÖ About Clas┬Łsi┬Łcal Antiq┬Łui┬Łty?,ŌĆØ they date back at least to 19th cen┬Łtu┬Łry philoso┬Łpher Lud┬Łwig Feuer┬Łbach, who called WinckelmannŌĆÖs ideas about Roman stat┬Łues ŌĆ£an emp┬Łty fig┬Łment of the imag┬Łi┬Łna┬Łtion.ŌĆØ But these ideas are ŌĆ£for the most part tak┬Łen for grant┬Łed rather than ques┬Łtioned,ŌĆØ Porter argues, ŌĆ£or else clung to for fear of los┬Łing a pow┬Łer┬Łful cachet that, even in the belea┬Łguered present, con┬Łtin┬Łues to trans┬Łlate into cul┬Łtur┬Łal pres┬Łtige, author┬Łi┬Łty, elit┬Łist sat┬Łis┬Łfac┬Łtions, and eco┬Łnom┬Łic pow┬Łer.ŌĆØ
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2019.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
Why Most Ancient Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtions Had No Word for the Col┬Łor Blue
Why Ancient Romans Paid a For┬Łtune for the Col┬Łor Pur┬Łple ŌĆö More Than Even Sil┬Łver
How Ancient Greek Stat┬Łues Real┬Łly Looked: Reseasrch Reveals Their Bold, Bright Col┬Łors and Pat┬Łterns
The Met Dig┬Łi┬Łtal┬Łly Restores the Col┬Łors of an Ancient Egypt┬Łian Tem┬Łple, Using Pro┬Łjec┬Łtion Map┬Łping Tech┬Łnol┬Ło┬Łgy
Watch Art on Ancient Greek Vas┬Łes Come to Life with 21st Cen┬Łtu┬Łry Ani┬Łma┬Łtion
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬Łcian based in Durham, NC.┬Ā

title: Grant Garmezy Molds a Full-Size Dakotaraptor from Molten Glass
description: Dakotaraptor, a fossilized skeleton of which was discovered a little more than 20 years ago by paleontologists in South Dakota, was an extremely l
body:
Dakotaraptor, a fossilized skeleton of which was discovered a little more than 20 years ago by paleontologists in South Dakota, was an extremely lethal prehistoric predator. Its feathered body, powerful legs, and huge jaw gave it an advantage as it roamed its territory some 66 million years ago. But it was really its so-called ŌĆ£sickle claw,ŌĆØ a huge, taloned toe that measures 9.5 inches on the outer curve. 
For artist Grant Garmezy, the ancient creature presented a unique opportunity to render a life-size sculptural version. Specializing in meticulously detailed, accurate representations of nature in glass, he took on the challenge of recreating the DakotaraptorŌĆÖs 14-foot length from snout to tail.

ŌĆ£The fossil record leaves much to interpretation, as details like feathers and posture remain a matter of scientific debate,ŌĆØ says a statement from GRANADA Gallery, which supported the artist in the project. ŌĆ£This uncertainty mirrors the artistry of the project itself, where each form is a choice, a vision, and a reimagining of prehistory.ŌĆØ
Go behind-the-scenes as Garmezy makes the sculpture on Instagram, and explore more of his process on YouTube.

schema.headline: Grant Garmezy Molds a Full-Size Dakotaraptor from Molten Glass

title: 'Lettres D├®coratives' Is a Celebration of Fin de Si├©cle Sign Painters' Vibrant Letterforms
description: Before digital fonts and the ability to reproduce graphics on a large scale, there were sign painters. Today, printers can spit out countless post
body:
Before digital fonts and the ability to reproduce graphics on a large scale, there were sign painters. Today, printers can spit out countless posters and ads, but there was a time when hand-painted promotional signage was needed for retail windows, and business names were often rendered just the same. Of course, itŌĆÖs a trade that virtually died out with the advent of new technologies, which made it cheaper and faster to produce public messaging. In the way of LPs and film cameras, though, just because there were new methods in daily use, it certainly doesnŌĆÖt mean that the art form doesnŌĆÖt live on. 
A new book published by Letterform Archive, Lettres D├®coratives: A Century of French Sign PaintersŌĆÖ Alphabets, celebrates the vivacity and timelessness of French sign painting from the 19th and early 20th centuries. Compiled from lithograph portfolios, which range from 1875 to around 1932, the volume includes more than 150 full-color reproductions of these bold lettering samples. These portfolios once served as catalogue-like albums, providing inspiration for styles and motifs that could be translated onto large billboards and small signage alike.

While decorative painting had been around long before embellished lettering came into vogue, it took a while to catch on. ŌĆ£Only in the nineteenth century did painters make alphabets into primary decorative elements,ŌĆØ writes sign painter Morgane C├┤me in the introduction. ŌĆ£Indeed even then, many of the letters painted on signboards, canvas awnings, and vehicles remained simple in form, following inherited models of Roman square capitals and modern variants used in printing type. The new demand for eye-catching signs called for significant change in perspective.ŌĆØ
Explore more in your own copy, which you can grab from the Colossal Shop.

schema.headline: 'Lettres D├®coratives' Is a Celebration of Fin de Si├©cle Sign Painters' Vibrant Letterforms

title: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects
description: In the age of the internet, weŌĆÖre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain
body:
In the age of the internet, weŌĆÖre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain and programs like The Metropolitan Museum of ArtŌĆÖs Open Access Initiative. Through a searchable digital catalogue, visitors to the museumŌĆÖs website can see hundreds of thousands of objects, many images of which are available for download. And itŌĆÖs not aloneŌĆöother institutions like the Art Institute of Chicago, The National Gallery of Art, and The Cleveland Museum of Art, among others, make pieces in their collections accessible to all.
The thing is, digital images donŌĆÖt always give us the full picture, so to speak. Even two-dimensional paintings and drawings have unique textures, structural details, and materials that we can only really appreciate in person. This wonŌĆÖt ever really changeŌĆönothing beats the real thing. But one caveat is that even in person, much of the work remains hidden. We canŌĆÖt see the backs of oil paintings, for example, and edges are often hidden within frames. Thanks to The MetŌĆÖs continued emphasis on imaging, we can now experience every detail in three-dimensional renderings of nearly 140 significant objects in its holdings.
The Temple of Dendur (10th century B.C.E.)
The Met is home to a whopping 1.5 million historic objects, which range from the iconic Temple of Dendur and Impressionist paintings to African tribal ceremonial sculptures and medieval pottery. The museum recently published 3D models of some of these, plus numerous other objects, including nine produced in collaboration with NHK (Japan Broadcasting Corporation).
With careful attention to technical precision and color, these animated renderings are research-grade tools, allowing us to see the objects at any angle. View van GoghŌĆÖs brushstrokes closer than youŌĆÖre allowed to in a museum, zoom in on a Babylonian cuneiform tablet, and turn an 18th-century Turkish tile over to see its reverse side.
The Met plans to continue adding 3D scans to its online library. Explore more on the museumŌĆÖs website.

schema.headline: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects

title: ├ülvaro Urbano Suspends Fleeting Moments of Decay in Metal Plants
description: Anyone whoŌĆÖs decried the seasonal blip we call autumn knows how rapidly nature can swing from verdant greenery to leafless branches. The same goes
body:
Anyone whoŌĆÖs decried the seasonal blip we call autumn knows how rapidly nature can swing from verdant greenery to leafless branches. The same goes for the missed watering of an overlooked houseplant: skip a week and bear witness to browning edges that curl into a crisp. As quickly as these natural changes occur, so do their remedies or downfalls, and soon weŌĆÖre spotting new buds or depositing the evidence of our negligence in the compost bin.
For ├ülvaro Urbano, the brief period between blossom and decay is one to be preserved. He sculpts common plants from metal, casting vulnerable life forms into a sturdy material and rendering their colors and textures in paint. ItŌĆÖs an act of making ŌĆ£small monuments of things that normally would disappear or change in a few days, or in minutes,ŌĆØ the artist says.
ŌĆ£TABLEAU VIVANT (Dogwood)ŌĆØ (2024), metal and paint, 175 x 125 x 88 centimeters. Photo by Marjorie Brunet Plaza, courtesy of the artist and Marian Goodman Gallery
Drawing on theater and architecture, Urbano is deeply interested in creating not only standalone works but immersive scenes. His sculptures often leave a trail of leaves on the floor or appear to grow directly from the stark gallery wall, their knotted branches jutting out into the space. ŌĆ£The viewer can enter these situations as if they are witnesses (to) a scene that has already started,ŌĆØ the artist adds.
Urbano lives and works between Paris and Berlin and has work on view in the latter at Spore Initiative. Find more on Instagram.
Installation view of ŌĆśA╠ülvaro Urbano: GRANADA GRANADAŌĆÖ (2023), Travesi╠ü a Cuatro, Mexico City. Photo by Ramiro Chaves, courtesy of the artist and Travesi╠ü a Cuatro
ŌĆ£Hotel Gazmira (Musa acuminata)ŌĆØ (2025), metal and paint, 200 x 35 x 37 centimeters. Photo by Marjorie Brunet Plaza, courtesy of the artist
Detail of ŌĆ£Hotel Gazmira (Musa acuminata)ŌĆØ (2025)
ŌĆ£In├®sŌĆØ (2025), metal and paint, 50 x 35 x 125 centimeters. Photo by Marjorie Brunet Plaza, courtesy of the artist
Installation view of ŌĆśA╠ülvaro Urbano: GRANADA GRANADAŌĆÖ (2023), Travesi╠ü a Cuatro, Mexico City. Photo by Ramiro Chaves, courtesy of the artist and Travesi╠ü a Cuatro
Installation view of ŌĆśA╠ülvaro Urbano: GRANADA GRANADAŌĆÖ (2023), Travesi╠ü a Cuatro, Mexico City. Photo by Ramiro Chaves, courtesy of the artist and Travesi╠ü a Cuatro

title: Olafur Eliasson: A symphony of disappearing sounds for the Great Salt Lake
description:
Announcement
                As the lakeŌĆÖs ecological crisis worsens, the artistŌĆÖs new site-specific installation in Salt Lake City r
body:
Announcement
                As the lakeŌĆÖs ecological crisis worsens, the artistŌĆÖs new site-specific installation in Salt Lake City renders audible what is increasingly at risk of vanishing.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Olafur Eliasson testing A symphony of disappearing sounds for the Great Salt Lake (2026); commissioned for Wake the Great Salt Lake; supported by Salt Lake City Art Council, Salt Lake City MayorŌĆÖs Office, and the Bloomberg Philanthropies Public Art Challenge (┬® 2026 Olafur Eliasson, photo courtesy Studio Olafur Eliasson)
    
        
            From March 26 through April 4, audiences are invited to experience A symphony of disappearing sounds for the Great Salt Lake, a new large-scale work by Icelandic-Danish artist Olafur Eliasson. Presented nightly in Salt Lake CityŌĆÖs Memory Grove, the project combines an original musical composition with a vivid light projection that unfolds after dark across the surface of a towering, elevated sphere.The electronic musical composition draws on field recordings of local wildlife and environmental phenomena, sourced from archival materials along with new recordings made specifically for the installation. By transporting the sounds of the lakeŌĆÖs ecosystem into an urban park setting, Eliasson foregrounds the fragile interdependence between human and more-than-human life, rendering audible what is increasingly at risk of vanishing.The installation marks EliassonŌĆÖs first work in the Intermountain West and serves as the culminating event of Wake the Great Salt Lake, a two-year public art initiative highlighting the lakeŌĆÖs rapid decline. Organized through a partnership between the Salt Lake City Arts Council, the Salt Lake City MayorŌĆÖs Office, and the Bloomberg Philanthropies Public Art Challenge, the initiative has supported temporary works that respond to the lakeŌĆÖs urgent crisis. In addition to EliassonŌĆÖs installation, Wake the Great Salt Lake has featured 12 projects by local artists and artists with Utah ties, channeling local concerns and hopes surrounding the lakeŌĆÖs future.Eliasson, whose practice has long examined perception and environmental awareness, is widely known for immersive public installations. His 2003 work, The weather project, featured a glowing indoor sun shrouded in mist at Tate ModernŌĆÖs Turbine Hall in London. In 2008, Eliasson constructed four expansive artificial waterfalls along the Manhattan and Brooklyn shorelines for The New York City Waterfalls. With Ice Watch, he transported blocks of glacial ice from Greenland to urban centers in Copenhagen, Paris, and London, inviting viewers to confront the material reality of climate change. In 2024, Eliasson broadcast Lifeworld on screens in iconic public spaces around the world. Commissioned by CIRCA, the short, blurred sequences transported passers-by into a space of uncertainty unlike the highly defined city spaces they are used to.Without action, the Great Salt LakeŌĆÖs collapse would have major ecological and economic impacts on the region. A symphony of disappearing sounds for the Great Salt Lake positions art as a site of collective listening and reflection ŌĆö an opportunity to consider what stands to be lost and what might yet be preserved.To learn more and RSVP, visit wakegsl.org.
                  
                      
                        Announcement
                      
                      
                        Sponsored
                      
                      
                        Event Announcement
                      
                      
                        Utah

title: Forget About Getting Your AI Art Copyrighted
description:
News
                The US Supreme Court declines to hear a years-long case on the matter, leaving one famous AI art crusader out in
body:
News
                The US Supreme Court declines to hear a years-long case on the matter, leaving one famous AI art crusader out in the cold. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            ŌĆ£A Recent Entrance to ParadiseŌĆØ is the AI-generated image at the center of this legal battle. ( DABUS, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons_
    
        
            Computer scientist Stephen Thaler struck out once again this week after the United States Supreme Court declined to hear his appeal on the years-long legal battle to secure copyright protections for an AI-generated ŌĆ£artwork.ŌĆØ Thaler, who has pushed to have an image developed by his AI system ŌĆ£Creativity MachineŌĆØ copyrighted since 2018, escalated the matter to the Supreme Court last year after lower courts repeatedly upheld the US Copyright Office's hardline decision to withhold registration.The agency ruled that the AI-generated image, titled ŌĆ£A Recent Entrance to ParadiseŌĆØ (2012), was not eligible for registration in 2019 because it didn't meet the Office's ŌĆ£human authorshipŌĆØ requirement. Thaler's 2020 and 2022 appeals to the agency were both rejected, and he filed a lawsuit against Shira Perlmutter, the register of copyrights and the director of the Copyright Office, shortly after the second rejection.  In 2023, the District Court judge presiding over the case argued that ŌĆ£human authorship is a bedrock requirement of copyright.ŌĆØ Undeterred, Thaler invoked the Court of Appeals, which also upheld the Copyright Office's decision in 2025.After multiple motions to extend the window of time to file a petition with the Supreme Court, Thaler and his legal counsel challenged the Court of Appeals' ruling last October. The petition garnered support from multiple professors and researchers who disagreed with the lower courts' interpretations of the Copyright Office's decision, arguing that ŌĆ£excluding AI-generated works from copyright protection threatens the foundations of American creativity, innovation, and economic growth.ŌĆØIt's worth noting that in 2023, the US Copyright Office produced a statement of policy outlining that some AI-generated artworks could be eligible for copyright protections and will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to discern if the AI elements used were the result of ŌĆ£mechanical reproductionŌĆØ or of an authorŌĆÖs ŌĆ£own original mental conception, to which [the author] gave visible form.ŌĆØHowever, the Supreme Court declined to hear this issue on Monday, March 2 ŌĆö another blow to Thaler's fight to update the parameters to qualify for copyright registration as technological advances develop at light speed. This development hasn't let the air out of Thaler's tires, who told CNET that he sees the Supreme Court's rejection ŌĆ£as a philosophical milestone rather than a defeat.ŌĆØ ŌĆ£The law is lagging behind what technology can already do,ŌĆØ Thaler told the tech media outlet, saying that this case confronted the theoretical definition of inventions as strictly human-made innovations. ŌĆ£The court addressed what the statute currently allows. It did not address what technology has already achieved.ŌĆØ
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        AI art

schema.headline: Forget About Getting Your AI Art Copyrighted
